The applicant was represented by Mr H.K. Elban and Ms Serpil Yalçın, lawyers practising in Istanbul. The Turkish Government (“the Government”) were represented by their Agent.

On 11 December 2006 the President of the Second Section decided to give notice of the application to the Government. It was also decided to examine the merits of the application at the same time as its admissibility (Article 29 § 3).

THE FACTS

I. THE CIRCUMSTANCES OF THE CASE

The applicant was born in 1941 and lives in Antalya.

The applicant owns a flat in Antalya. Based on a construction permit issued by the Antalya Municipality on 14 February 1991, construction works were commenced on neighbouring land to build an apartment block. The limitations concerning the height of the building were not respected and, as a result, the sea view from the applicant’s flat, which is situated on the sixth floor, was blocked by the new building.

On an unspecified date, the applicant initiated administrative proceedings against the Antalya Municipality for the annulment of the construction permit. He maintained that the permit was in breach of city planning regulations. On 27 June 1995 the Antalya Administrative Court found in the applicant’s favour and annulled the construction permit. On 13 March 1996 the Supreme Administrative Court upheld the decision of the Antalya Administrative Court.

The applicant asked the Antalya Municipality to enforce the decision of the Antalya Administrative Court, but his request was rejected.

On 18 October 1998 the applicant initiated compensation proceedings before the Antalya Administrative Court on account of the Municipality’s failure to implement the court order of 13 March 1996. He requested both pecuniary and non-pecuniary compensation, stating that the non-enforcement of the final court decision which had annulled the construction permit had diminished his flat’s value.

On 19 November 1998 the Antalya Administrative Court partially upheld the applicant’s claim, awarding him pecuniary compensation, but refusing non-pecuniary damages.

On 26 April 2000 the Supreme Administrative Court upheld the Antalya Administrative Court’s decision in respect of pecuniary damage. However, finding that the applicant was also entitled to non-pecuniary compensation, it quashed the judgment in this respect. Subsequent to this decision, the Municipality paid the pecuniary compensation to the applicant.

On 1 November 2000 the Antalya Administrative Court awarded 250,000,000 Turkish liras (TRL) to the applicant in respect of non-pecuniary damage.

On 16 April 2002, considering the amount of non-pecuniary compensation insufficient, the Supreme Administrative Court quashed the judgment of the Antalya Administrative Court once again.

On 19 December 2002 the Antalya Administrative Court insisted on maintaining its judgment. The applicant appealed.

On 16 June 2005 the Supreme Administrative Court quashed the judgment of the Antalya Administrative Court of 19 December 2002.

On 29 December 2005 the Antalya Administrative Court adhered to the decision of the Supreme Administrative Court and awarded the applicant TRL 1,000,000,000 in respect of non-pecuniary compensation with statutory interest running from 18 October 1996.

On 27 June 2006 the Supreme Administrative Court rejected the appeal of the Municipality. The Municipality paid the due amount to the applicant.

PROCEDURE

The case originated in an application (no. 48247/06) against the Republic of Poland lodged with the Court under Article 34 of the Convention for the Protection of Human Rights and Fundamental Freedoms (“the Convention”) by a Polish national, Mr Grzegorz Dublas (“the applicant”), on 10 November 2006.

The Polish Government were represented by their Agent, Mr J. Wołąsiewicz of the Ministry of Foreign Affairs.

The applicant alleged, inter alia, that the length of his pre-trial detention and of criminal proceedings against him had exceeded a “reasonable time” within the meaning of Articles 5 § 3 and 6 § 1 of the Convention.

On 30 August 2007 the President of the Fourth Section decided to communicate the complaints concerning the length of the applicant’s pre‑trial detention and of his criminal proceedings to the Government. It was also decided to examine the merits of the application at the same time as its admissibility (Article 29 § 3 of the Convention).

THE FACTS

I. THE CIRCUMSTANCES OF THE CASE

The applicant was born in 1972 and lives in Pruszcz Gdański.

On 8 June 2004 he was arrested by the police.

On 9 June 2004 the Gdańsk District Court (Sąd Rejonowy) decided to place the applicant in pre-trial detention as there were reasonable grounds for suspecting that he had been dealing in drugs. The court also cited the risk that the applicant would go into hiding, in view of the likelihood of a heavy sentence.

On 2 September and 4 November 2004 the applicant’s detention was extended. In addition to the two grounds given previously, the District Court cited the need to ensure the proper conduct of the investigation.

On 26 October 2004 the applicant was indicted before the Gdańsk District Court. His pre-trial detention was subsequently extended.

As the length of the applicant’s detention had reached the statutory time‑limit of two years laid down in Article 263 § 3 of the Code of Criminal Procedure (Kodeks postępowania karnego), the District Court made a series of applications to the Gdańsk Court of Appeal (Sąd Apelacyjny) for the applicant’s detention to be extended beyond that term. On 18 May and 17 August 2006 the Gdańsk Court of Appeal granted the applications and extended his detention, citing the reasonable suspicion against the applicant and the length of the sentence that was likely to be imposed. The Court also considered that the District Court could not be held responsible for not having completed the trial as there had been objective procedural difficulties in hearing all the witnesses.

The first hearing was held on 21 January 2005. Subsequently, hearings were held at regular intervals.

The applicant’s applications for release from detention and his appeals against the decisions extending the preventive measure were dismissed.

On 17 October 2006 the Gdańsk Court of Appeal extended the applicant’s detention until 20 November 2006. It cited the reasonable suspicion against the applicant and found that detention was necessary to secure the proper conduct of the proceedings given the likelihood of a heavy sentence.

The applicant’s detention on remand was not extended beyond that date and on 20 November 2006 he was released.

On 4 August 2006 the applicant lodged a complaint under section 5 of the Law of 17 June 2004 on complaints about a breach of the right to a trial within a reasonable time (Ustawa o skardze na naruszenie prawa strony do rozpoznania sprawy w postępowaniu sądowym bez nieuzasadnionej zwłoki) (“the 2004 Act”). On 3 November 2006 the Gdańsk Regional Court dismissed the complaint, finding that the trial had been conducted speedily and with no periods of inactivity.

The proceedings are still pending before the trial court.

PROCEDURE

The case originated in an application (no. 77746/01) against the Republic of Poland lodged with the Court under Article 34 of the Convention for the Protection of Human Rights and Fundamental Freedoms (“the Convention”) by a Polish national, Mrs Waleria Kroenitz (“the applicant”), on 28 July 2000.

The Polish Government (“the Government”) were represented by their Agent, Mr K. Drzewicki, of the Ministry of Foreign Affairs. The applicant was exceptionally granted leave to be represented by Mr Cz. Sławski (Rule 36 § 4 of the Rules of Court). The latter was also granted leave to use the Polish language in the proceedings before the Court (Rule 34 § 3 of the Rules of Court).

The applicant alleged in particular, that her right to a “hearing within reasonable time” had not been respected.

The application was allocated to the Fourth Section of the Court. Within that Section, the Chamber that would consider the case (Article 27 § 1 of the Convention) was constituted as provided in Rule 26 § 1 of the Rules of Court. On 30 April the Fourth Section decided to communicate the complaint concerning the length of the proceedings and an alleged interference with the applicant’s property rights to the Government. Under the provisions of Article 29 § 3 of the Convention, it decided to examine the merits of the application at the same time as its admissibility. It also gave priority to application, pursuant to Rule 41 of the Rules of the Court.

THE FACTS

The applicant, Mrs Waleria Kroenitz, is a Polish national, who was born in 1903 and lives in Przemyśl, Poland.

A. Proceedings concerning a claim for restitution and compensation.

In 1948 the applicant’s property was expropriated. On 28 June 1996 the Minister of Economy (Minister Gospodarki) annulled the expropriation order.

On 23 December 1996 the applicant brought a claim against a company “Domgos” (Częstochowskie Zakłady Metalowe “Domgos”) before the Częstochowa Regional Court (Sąd Wojewódzki), seeking restitution of her property. The court held hearings on 30 May, 10 July and 17 October 1997.

On 30 January 1998 the applicant modified her claim and filed an action for compensation for loss of profits caused by the prolonged impossibility of using her property.

On 8 March 1999 the Częstochowa Regional Court held a hearing and gave an interlocutory judgment. It ordered that the property be restored to the applicant.

On 9 December 1999 the court was to hold a hearing, but an expert failed to appear because he had not been duly summoned. On 27 January 2000 and 23 March 2000 the court held further hearings.

A hearing listed for 15 February 2001 was cancelled since the expert and the defendant failed to appear.

The hearing listed for 29 March 2001 was adjourned as the court had ordered the expert to prepare a supplementary report. On 21 February 2002 the court held a hearing.

On 8 March 2002 the Regional Court gave judgment and awarded the applicant compensation. Following the defendant’s appeal, the proceedings are pending before the Court of Appeal (Sąd Apelacyjny).

B. Proceedings concerning a claim for compensation against the State Treasury.

On 26 April 1998 the applicant asked the Minister of Economy to award her compensation. On 22 May 1998 the Minister dismissed her request.

On 24 June 1998 the applicant filed with the Warsaw Regional Court an action for compensation against the State Treasury the Minister of Economy. The court held hearings on 19 November 1999 and 22 September 2000.

On 22 September 2000 the court gave judgment and dismissed the applicant’s claim. The applicant appealed.

On 24 January and 7 March 2002 the Warsaw Court of Appeal (Sąd Apelacyjny) held hearings. On the latter date the court gave judgment. The applicant did not lodge a cassation appeal with the Supreme Court (Sąd Najwyższy).

PROCEDURE

The case originated in an application (no. 27833/02) against the Republic of Poland lodged with the Court under Article 34 of the Convention for the Protection of Human Rights and Fundamental Freedoms (“the Convention”) by a Polish national, Mr Artur Nowak (“the applicant”), on 17 July 2002.

The Polish Government (“the Government”) were represented by their Agents, Ms S. Jaczewska, and subsequently, Mr J. Wołąsiewicz of the Ministry of Foreign Affairs.

On 5 June 2003 the President of the Fourth Section decided to communicate the complaint concerning the length of the proceedings to the Government. Under the provisions of Article 29 § 3 of the Convention, it was decided to examine the merits of the application at the same time as its admissibility.

THE FACTS

The applicant was born in 1976 and lives in Piotrków Trybunalski, Poland.

In December 1993 the applicant, at that time seventeen years old, was hit by a car driven by a certain Mr R.Z. As a consequence of the accident, his left leg had to be amputated.

On 17 June 1994 the applicant, represented by his mother, lodged with the Piotrków Regional Court (Sąd Wojewódzki) a civil action against the State Insurance Company (Powszechny Zakład Ubezpieczeń). He claimed 800,000,000 old zlotys in compensation for pecuniary and non‑pecuniary damage.

On 18 June 1994 the trial court exempted the applicant from payment of court-fees.

On 24 August 1994 the first hearing was held. The trial court requested the applicant's medical file from a hospital and ordered an expert opinion.

At the next hearing held on 1 February 1995 the court heard an expert witness and requested a second expert opinion.

Between 2 February and 21 November 1995 no hearings were held.

At the hearing held on 22 November 1995 the trial court gave the parties a time-limit for reaching a friendly settlement.

Subsequently, hearings were held on 11 March and 27 November 1996. At those hearings the court heard the applicant's mother and an expert witness.

The hearing scheduled for 26 February 1997 was cancelled due to illness of the applicant's counsel.

On 27 February 1997 the applicant modified his claim. He in addition sought a monthly allowance.

Afterwards, hearings were held on 5 March and 9 April 1997. Mr. R.Z., the driver responsible for the accident, joined the proceedings as an intervener.

Between 10 April 1997 and 10 September 1998 no hearings were held.

On 11 September and 9 October 1998 the trial court held hearings at which it heard an expert witness and the applicant.

On 2 December 1998 the Piotrków Trybunalski Regional Court gave judgment. The trial court awarded the applicant compensation and a monthly pension.

On 28 January 1999 the defendant lodged an appeal against this judgment.

On 16 April 1999 the Łódź Court of Appeal (Sąd Apelacyjny) held a hearing and gave judgment. It amended the first-instance judgment regarding interest and ordered the applicant to reimburse the costs of the proceedings incurred by the defendant.

On 1 June 1999 the applicant lodged a cassation appeal with the Supreme Court (Sąd Najwyższy).

On 23 January 2002 the Supreme Court held a hearing and gave judgment. The Supreme Court partly allowed his cassation appeal.

PROCEDURE

The case originated in an application (no. 17725/07) against the Republic of Turkey lodged with the Court under Article 34 of the Convention for the Protection of Human Rights and Fundamental Freedoms (“the Convention”) by a Turkish national, Mr Necmettin Alkan (“the applicant”), on 11 April 2007.

The applicant was represented by Ms L. Alkan, his mother. The Turkish Government (“the Government”) were represented by their Agent.

The applicant alleged, in particular, that his right of access to court had been breached by the domestic court’s refusal to grant him legal aid.

On 25 May 2010 the President of the Second Section decided to give notice of the application to the Government. It was also decided to rule on the admissibility and merits of the application at the same time (Article 29 § 1).

THE FACTS

I. THE CIRCUMSTANCES OF THE CASE

The applicant was born in 1979 and lives in Kars.

On 13 December 2002, after having completed his military service, the applicant was placed under the guardianship of his father, S.A., by a decision of the Sarıkamış Magistrates’ Court as he was mentally ill and needed constant supervision.

The applicant submitted several requests to the gendarmerie command claiming compensation for his illness which, according to him, began during his military service as a result of the treatment there. His requests were rejected on the basis of a medical report from the Gülhane Military Medical Academy, which indicated that such a condition could not have been caused merely by military service and that many other factors could have been involved in its development.

Subsequently, under the guardianship of S.A., the applicant initiated compensation proceedings before the Supreme Military Administrative Court, claiming that his illness had been caused by the ill-treatment to which he had been subjected in the army. He argued that his enrolment for military service after having passed several medical tests was proof that he had been healthy before. The applicant requested a total of 200,000 Turkish liras (TRY) (approximately 100,000 euros (EUR) at the time) in respect of both pecuniary and non-pecuniary damage, submitting the medical records from several hospitals in support of his claims. He also requested legal aid for the court fees, presenting a certificate from the office of the headman (muhtar) attesting to his father’s poverty. The certificate stated that neither S.A. nor anyone else from his household was employed. It also indicated that he was ill, had no social security and depended on support from his neighbours.

By two decisions of 19 July and 1 November 2006 respectively, the Supreme Military Administrative Court rejected the applicant’s legal aid request, stating that pursuant to the Code of Civil Procedure, a legal aid request must be supported by proof that the case is well-founded and that the plaintiff does not have the means to pay the required court fees. It concluded that the requirements had not been met in the applicant’s case.

On 6 November 2006 the court informed the applicant that he was to pay a total of TRY 2,719 (approximately EUR 1,400 at the time) in court fees within thirty days for the proceedings to be continued.

On 10 January 2007 the Supreme Military Administrative Court decided to discontinue the compensation proceedings because the applicant had not paid the necessary court fees.

PROCEDURE

The case originated in an application (no. 71893/01) against the Republic of Poland lodged with the Court under Article 34 of the Convention for the Protection of Human Rights and Fundamental Freedoms (“the Convention”) by a Polish national, Mr Tadeusz Cegielski (“the applicant”), on 9 May 2000.

The Polish Government (“the Government”) were represented by their Agent, Mr K. Drzewicki, of the Ministry of Foreign Affairs.

On 14 January 2003 the Court decided to communicate the complaint concerning the length of the proceedings and the alleged lack of effective remedy to the Government. Under the provisions of Article 29 § 3 of the Convention, it decided to examine the merits of the application at the same time as its admissibility.

THE FACTS

The applicant was born in 1933 and lives in Bielsko-Biała, Poland.

In 1993 the Szczecin Regional Court annulled the decision given in 1947 by the Szczecin Military Court which had convicted the applicant’s father and had sentenced him to imprisonment and the forfeiture of his property.

On 25 September 1995 the Szczecin Regional Court (Criminal Chamber) awarded the applicant compensation for his father’s imprisonment.

On 20 July 1995 the applicant initiated before the Szczecin Regional Court (Sąd Wojwódzki w Szczecinie) civil proceedings against the State Treasury. He claimed compensation for the forfeited estate and farm machinery.

On 25 September 1995, upon the trial court’s request, the applicant called as defendant the State Treasury represented by the Bydgoszcz Regional Court.

In January 1996 the trial court requested the Criminal Chamber of the same court to provide the applicant’s case-file. It received the file in September 1996.

On 10 February 1999 the trial court held the first hearing at which it heard the applicant. It further decided that three witnesses would be heard by the Myslibórz District Court.

On 23 March and 13 April 1999 the Myslibórz District Court held hearings at which it heard the witnesses.

On 4 August 1999 the applicant applied to the trial court to give a partial judgment relating only to the pecuniary damage sustained by his father in respect of loss of income which, according to him, was fully justified by the provided documents.

On 14 February 2000 the trial court joined as a co-defendant the Minister of Finance. Three months later the trial court joined as another co‑defendant the Minister of the Treasury.

On 6 September 2000 the court held hearing at which it asked for an expert opinion concerning the value of the farm machinery.

On 8 May 2001 the expert opinion was submitted to the trial court.

On 18 May 2001 the court requested another expert opinion concerning the value of the land and buildings.

On 17 October 2001 the trial court held a hearing.

On 29 October 2001 the Szczecin Regional Court gave a partial judgment. It awarded the applicant PLN 21,105 as compensation for the confiscated machinery. The remaining part of the action was to be examined at the later date. The Minister of the Treasury appealed against this judgment.

On 21 May 2002 the Poznań Court of Appeal dismissed the Minister’s appeal against the Szczecin Regional Court’s partial judgment. The Minister of the Treasury lodged a cassation appeal with the Supreme Court. This part of the proceedings is pending before the Supreme Court.

The proceedings relating to the part of the applicant’s action which was not examined by the Szczecin Regional Court in its partial judgment, are still pending before the trial court.

PROCEDURE

The case originated in an application (no. 24203/05) against the Republic of Poland lodged with the Court under Article 34 of the Convention for the Protection of Human Rights and Fundamental Freedoms (“the Convention”) by a Polish national, Mr Jarosław Kyzioł (“the applicant”), on 17 June 2005.

The Polish Government (“the Government”) were represented by their Agent, Mr J. Wołąsiewicz of the Ministry of Foreign Affairs.

On 7 December 2006 the President of the Fourth Section of the Court decided to give notice of the application to the Government. Applying Article 29 § 3 of the Convention, it was decided to rule on the admissibility and merits of the application at the same time.

THE FACTS

I. THE CIRCUMSTANCES OF THE CASE

The applicant was born in 1969 and lives in Cracow. 1 Civil proceedings for dissolution of co-ownership

On 28 October 1994 a housing co-operative filed with the Cracow District Court (Sąd Rejonowy) a claim for dissolution of co-ownership of five plots of land. The applicant is one of the co-owners of the land in question and has his family house there. There were 182 parties to the proceedings at the time when they were first instituted. This number has since increased to 417.

On 16 October 1997 the Cracow Regional Court (Sąd Okręgowy) decided to join the case in question to a related case involving a claim for the ascertainment of the ownership right to the same land, and to examine them together.

On 1 March 1999 the President of the Cracow District Court appointed an expert in geodesy. On 19 July 1999 the court admitted his expert report as evidence. On 1 July 2002 the President of the Cracow District Court requested that the original report be updated. The additional expert report was furnished in June 2003.

On 20 November and 18 December 2003 respectively the court decided to exclude two judges from dealing with the case. Moreover, on 18 December 2003 the Cracow District Court decided of its own motion to stay the proceedings due to the deaths of several of the parties, the need to ascertain the shares of their legal heirs and to summon new parties.

On 13 April 2006 the Cracow District Court resumed the proceedings since the successors of the deceased parties had been summoned to participate. However, in the same decision the court decided to stay the proceedings due to new circumstances, namely the deaths of other parties.

2. Proceedings under the 2004 Act

On 15 December 2005 the applicant lodged a complaint about the unreasonable length of proceedings under the Act of 17 June 2004 on complaints about a breach of the right to a trial within a reasonable time (Ustawa o skardze na naruszenie prawa strony do rozpoznania sprawy w postępowaniu sądowym bez nieuzasadnionej zwłoki) (“the 2004 Act”).

On 14 February 2006 the Cracow Regional Court dismissed the complaint. The court held that the 2004 Act did not have retroactive effect and, consequently it examined the applicant’s claim only in respect of the period between the entry into force of the 2004 Act on 17 September 2004 and the date on which the complaint had been lodged by the applicant. The Cracow Regional Court found that the proceedings remained stayed due to the objective obstacle posed by the deaths of several of the parties and the delays caused by their legal heirs in submitting inheritance titles. The domestic court also emphasised that there was a considerable and ever-changing number of parties to the proceedings.

PROCEDURE

The cases originated in two applications (nos. 27080/08 and 40982/08) against the Republic of Turkey lodged with the Court under Article 34 of the Convention for the Protection of Human Rights and Fundamental Freedoms (“the Convention”) by two Turkish nationals, Mr Metin Köksal and Mr Servet Durdu (“the applicants”), on 29 May and 15 August 2008, respectively. The first applicant, Mr Köksal, was represented by Mr H. Çakıroğlu, a lawyer practising in Samsun. The second applicant, Mr Durdu, was represented by Mr M. Bayat, a lawyer practising in Ankara. The Turkish Government (“the Government”) were represented by their Agent.

On 13 May 2009 the President of the Second Section decided to give notice of the applications to the Government. It was also decided to examine the merits of the applications at the same time as their admissibility (Article 29 § 3).

THE FACTS

I. THE CIRCUMSTANCES OF THE CASES

The applicants were born in 1973 and 1965, respectively, and live in Samsun and Ankara.

A. Application no. 27080/08

On 17 October 2001 the applicant was discharged from his civil service post following his previous criminal conviction by a martial law court. The conviction had been finalised by the Military Court of Cassation's decision of 11 November 1997.

On 4 January 2002 the applicant brought an action before the Samsun Administrative Court to annul the discharge order.

On 5 June 2002 the Samsun Administrative Court rejected the applicant's request.

On 7 February 2005 the Supreme Administrative Court upheld the judgment of the Samsun Administrative Court. The written opinions of the public prosecutor and of the judge rapporteur (“tetkik hakimi”) concerning his appeal request were not communicated to the applicant.

On 31 December 2007 the Supreme Administrative Court dismissed the applicant's request for rectification of its previous decision.

B. Application no. 40982/08

On 11 January 2002 the applicant was discharged from his civil service post after a disciplinary decision. Criminal proceedings were also commenced against the applicant on the same grounds as those which had led to his discharge.

On 15 March 2002 the applicant brought an action before the Ankara Administrative Court to annul the discharge order.

On 25 June 2002 the Ankara Assize Court acquitted the applicant of the offences with which he had been charged. On 22 March 2004 the Court of Cassation upheld the applicant's acquittal.

In the meantime, on 31 March 2003 the Ankara Administrative Court refused the applicant's request to annul the discharge order. Relying on Section 131 of the Law on Civil Servants (Law no. 657), the administrative court held that the applicant's acquittal of the criminal charges did not absolve him from the disciplinary sanction.

On 26 April 2005 the Supreme Administrative Court upheld the judgment of the Ankara Administrative Court. The written opinions of the public prosecutor and of the judge rapporteur concerning his appeal request were not communicated to the applicant.

On 23 January 2008 the Supreme Administrative Court dismissed the applicant's rectification request. The final decision was served on the applicant on 4 March 2008.

The Grand Chamber of the European Court of Human Rights today delivered a significant ruling in the case of X v. After careful consideration of the evidence, the Court found in favor of the applicants, ruling that the measures employed by the respondent state fell short of the required standards, and therefore substantiated a violation of their right to a fair trial. The European Court of Human Rights recently heard a landmark case regarding the rights of individuals with disabilities in accessing public spaces, including transportation facilities. The applicants, a group of four individuals, filed a formal complaint against the state regarding the inadequate accessibility of public transportation in their local area. They argued that the existing infrastructure did not meet the standards set forth by the UN Convention on the Rights of Persons with Disabilities, thereby preventing them from fully participating in the social and economic life of their community. The applicants requested that the Court take urgent measures to ensure the necessary modifications are made to the transportation facilities, including the installation of ramps, widening of pathways, and provision of accessible public restrooms. The Court noted the complex and varied situations presenting themselves in Member States, acknowledging that each country's unique circumstances and resources often hinder the implementation of accessible facilities. However, the Court emphasized that the absence of such accommodations does not justify a violation of the applicants' rights under the Convention. To achieve full accessibility, the Court ordered the state to submit a detailed plan of action, including timelines and milestones for the necessary modifications, to be reported back to the Court within six months.

The European Court of Human Rights made a landmark ruling today, finding that a Polish woman's right to life in the "European mainland" had been infringed upon by her treatment whilst in detention from an unreported sex assault.
It is not in the jurisdiction of the European Court of Human Rights to impugn an individual's liberty solely on the basis of their being an 'existential' threat to immigration control.

The ruling of the court was upheld on appeal, although it was somewhat narrow and perfunctory, as the majority felt that the applicant had not provided sufficient evidence to prove a direct link between the breach of duty by the local authorities and the applicant's detention. In considering the broader context, particularly the tumultuous nature of the protests and the subsequent attempts at violence from rival groups, the court ultimately ruled in favor of the respondent, finding that the detention was a proportional response to the exceptional circumstances of the time.

"The Court of Grand Chamber, Seignanage Ltd. v. France (No. 12140/09), holding that the restriction imposed on advertising on paid-for time in French digital terrestrial television is inconsistent with Article 11 of the European Convention on the protection of human rights and fundamental freedoms, did not serve a legitimate aim necessary in a democratic society."

"Human rights should be viewed as universal values that have been deeply ingrained in the fabric of every civilized society, transcending national borders and cultural differences. In the context of the European Court of Human Rights, it is the role of the judiciary to ensure that individuals' fundamental rights are being protected and upheld, as stated in the European Convention on Human Rights."

"The European Court of Human Rights has consistently ruled that a person's freedom of expression does not extend to calling into question the democratic legitimacy of a government, as such critiques are necessary for a functioning democracy, yet in recent years the boundaries of tolerated speech have become increasingly contentious, with some governments invoking so-called 'national security' or 'public order' provisions to curtail freedom of expression, sparking heated debates among human rights advocates who argue that such laws inevitably undermine the fundamental right to freedom of assembly and association."

"Regarding the applicant's claim of arbitrary detention, the Commission notes that the ECHR Article 5, paragraph 1 - right to liberty and security, has been carefully considered in line with the principles of reasonable grounds. The Court of Session has determined that while the applicant was not informed of its legal representatives' right to legal counsel, despite holding in a different line of precedents.

"The applicant, a British national, filed a complaint against the state of France, alleging that his human rights had been violated due to the denial of a fair trial when extradition proceedings against him had been expedited in breach of Article 6 of the European Convention on Human Rights. The complainant's lawyer pointed out that critical aspects of evidence had not been disclosed to his defense team, thus prejudicing the verdict, and requested that the Court review and interpret the applicable law in its judgment to establish a minimum standard for the protection of the rights of individuals throughout the justice process in the interests of securing respect for human dignity."

The European Court of Human Rights has consistently upheld the right to freedom of expression, ruling in two separate cases last year that critics of the Iraqi government cannot prohibit internet users from calling for terrorism. In a landmark decision in the cases A. and B., the Grand Chamber holds that Article 10 of the European Convention on Human Rights does not permit the restriction of free speech merely because an internet forum or meeting may lead to group incitement of hatred.

"A thorough review of the relevant case law under Article 5 of the European Convention on Human Rights reveals that the prosecution's denial of bail to Mr. Kuznetsov as a suspected terrorist is problematic, as it raises concerns about the safeguard of his right to liberty and due process, precipitating an appeal to ensure that such measures are not tantamount to arbitrariness and violate the principles of equivalence and non-discrimination enshrined in Article 14 of the Convention."

"The Applicant, Mrs. Lefebvre, appealed against the decision of the District Court in Lefebvre v. The State at the first instance, where she had brought an application under Article 8 of the European Convention on Human Rights allegingthat the detention of a close family member for immigration, was not in line with her own history of psychiatric troubles and so it amounted to a violation of her Article 8 right in breach of Article 3 – the detention was deemed a violation and compensation awarded."

The European Court of Human Rights, having heard the application introduced by Mr. X, a UK national, alleging violations of his right to liberty and security in Denmark, considering the circumstances of his unlawful detention by Danmarks Retsforstandars (Supreme Court of Denmark) and that he had been subjected to physical violence. He maintained that his rights under Article 5 of the ECHR had not been protected and that the Danish authorities should be forced to compensate him for the damage sustained.

"The European Court of Human Rights' decision in the case of X v. Y, having examined the assertion that the defendant's arrest was permissible under Article 8 of the European Convention on Human Rights, found that the security measures implemented by the respondent state constituted a proportionate means of ensuring public order, the court reiterated that the detention of the applicant was compatible with Article 8, and subsequently released the applicant from custody on bail.

In the landmark case of Janda-Müller v. Germany, the European Court of Human Rights explicitly recognized the fundamental right to physical integrity and private life by emphasizing that detention without reasonable delay at the beginning of the criminal proceedings is arbitrary. The court ruled that the violation of Article 8 of the ECHR as interpreted in Kaur v. Latvia also applies to cases where a person has been held in detention for more than 16 hours, as is commonly the case in cases where police conduct post-charge detentions lasting overnight at official stations.

The European Court of Human Rights, in its landmark decision in the case of Bankovs vs. Latvia, underscored the importance of balancing individual freedoms with national security concerns. The applicant, Mr. Bankovs, had been detained for an indefinite period on grounds of national security, as he was deemed a potential threat to public order due to his involvement in extremist activities. However, his lawyer argued that the detention was illegal under Article 18 of the European Convention, citing the lack of due process and reasonable grounds. The court ultimately ruled in favor of Mr. Bankovs, stating that a broader definition of 'threat to public security' was necessary to ensure that no innocent individuals were unfairly detained, striking a delicate balance between national security concerns and the protection of human rights.

"The European Court of Human Rights has reaffirmed that Article 8 rights of a citizen are not subsumed by his individual human rights under a constitution because in some rare cases national constitutional principles may prevail unless those principles are incompatible with European human rights law establishing a legal context of protection more comprehensive than the rights afforded under the domestic Constitution, accordingly shaping a common judicial framework; this creates legal certainty amidst national constitutional arrangements."

"Ladies and gentlemen, your Eminence has announced that it is their decided that Article 8 of the European Convention on Human Rights, Section 1 regarding respect for private and family life and its sub-section 3 to safeguard exceptional measures for compelling moral reasons of public policy in areas of public health and public security, was indeed breached by the British governmental decision to segregate the applicant, thus violating the applicants' fundamental right to be recognized and understood as one indivisible family."

The European Court of Human Rights ruled unanimously in a landmark case that while freedom of expression is a fundamental right, it is not absolute, and there are limits to tolerance. In the judgment, the court held that the defendant's statements, although not directly involving the claimant, were relevant to her complaint as they reflected the prevailing public opinion against her in the community, thereby amounting to invidious discrimination. The court emphasized that the right to freedom of expression is not a license to incite hatred or xenophobia, but rather a means for the free discussion of ideas and opinions essential for a functioning democracy. Notably, in its application of Article 10, the court emphasized that the protection of minorities is also an essential aspect of the state's obligation to ensure protection under Article 3 of the European Convention on Human Rights.

In recent years, the European Court of Human Rights has established a significant body of jurisprudence on the topic of freedom of expression, clarifying the boundaries between an individual's right to freedom of expression and the protection of third-party interests. The Court has consistently emphasized the importance of balancing the free flow of information with the need to protect vulnerable groups from harm, often utilizing the case of Seerin v. the United Kingdom as a pivotal example of how the balancing test shapes the development of this right. This fundamental principle is further reinforced by the court's rejection of limits on freedom of expression in the context of political speeches delivered in Europe's predominantly homogeneous societies, highlighting the delicate dance between promoting social cohesion and safeguarding dissenting voices.

In a landmark decision on recent gender-discrimination allegations, the ECHR ruled that a private employer's refusal of a job candidate solely due to her sex constituted unjustified discrimination, even when that policy was ostensibly neutral and did not explicitly target a specific group.

The European Court of Human Rights has consistently held that the imposition of excessive bail amounts can undermine the fundamental right to a fair and impartial trial, as enshrined in Article 6 of the ECHR. In the case of Smith v. United Kingdom, the court ruled that the application of arbitrary bail conditions, including the imposition of high bail amounts, can serve as a form of ill-treatment contravening the right to a fair trial. This line of jurisprudence emphasizes the delicate balance between ensuring public safety and adhering to the principles of fairness in the administration of justice.

The European Court of Human Rights recently clarified the application of Article 2 in cases involving the killing of individuals under mysterious circumstances in circumstances amounting almost or entirely to torture. The Committee considered the aggravating factor of the involvement of corrupt or sub-ordinates officials and concluded the failure to exercise the duty to investigate when the victims' relatives presented as corroborative evidence of the violations at domestic level.

The European Court of Human Rights today once again heard the case of A & B against the United Kingdom, presenting a contentious issue of ageism in employment. The applicants claimed that their employer had unfairly dismissed them due to their advanced age, stating that their physical limitations no longer suited the company's modern work environment. The court weighed the arguments of both parties, examining the balance between the employer's needs and the employees' rights under the Framework Decision 2000/78 on equal treatment in employment and occupation based on age. The ECSR emphasized that age should not be a deciding factor, and the dismissals should have been subject to rigorous assessments. Given the overwhelming burden of proof, the court was compelled to consider whether the employer's actions were indeed arbitrary or in line with its own employment policies.

The Court of Human Rights, recognizing the inherent dignity and worth of every individual, reiterated its strong stance against any form of discrimination based on sex after the recent application, which involved a woman claiming unfair treatment in the workplace due to her gender.

In landmark proceedings before the European Court of Human Rights, the applicant alleged grave and irreparable harm would be inflicted upon their personal and family if the enforcement of domestic laws was not ensured, consequently jeopardizing fundamental rights and freedoms.

In the ongoing deliberations of the European Court of Human Rights, the plaintiff in Case 13338/15 brought forth a claim under Article 6 § 1 of the European Convention on Human Rights, subsection a, asserting that the admissibility of the search of her hotel room, in accordance with the UK version of the Youth Justice and Criminal Evidence Act 1999 and its interpretation, contravened Article 8 of the Convention, since the evidence obtained through this search had directly influenced the verdict rendered in a charged offense arising out of the case.

"The European Court of Human Rights has ruled that Bulgaria's failure to provide adequate compensation to a migrant who suffered life-changing injuries due to a tragic bus accident was in breach of Article 3, as the state failed to provide such compensation despite statutory obligations."

It is to be noted, however, that the reasoning behind the proposed compensation constituted a manifest injustice, since the family had been prevented from seeking compensation in their home jurisdiction due to restrictions placed upon them under the local legal system. Furthermore, a review of European Court of Human Rights case law has highlighted the critical need for comprehensive access to justice, be it within European borders or in relation to citizens seeking state accountability when relocating to other nations. The imposition of blanket restrictions upon family reunification, in this instance, runs afoul of Convention Article 3, which states that no one shall be expelled or returned to the frontiers of the State 'to be punished for an Act in which they have not been guilty.'

"In evaluating the ECHR's handling of its jurisdiction in the X case, the court's reliance on expert testimony was scrutinized, as it weighed the necessity of such input in evaluating the UK's obligations under the European Convention on Human Rights."

"The European Court of Human Rights has jurisdiction to consider claims based on general principles of human dignity and fundamental rights, independent of the specific provision of the actual legal instrument which has been breached."

"The President of the European Court of Human Rights today delivered a landmark judgment in case Băcău v. Romania, clarifying the intersection of European Convention on Human Rights and the European Convention against Trafficking in Human Organs. This ruling has significant implications for national systems addressing organ trafficking, upholding fundamental human rights and fostering international cooperation to safeguard the rights of individuals."

"A finding against an individual alleging that the applicant's home, being searched by Croatian police on the basis of reasonable suspicion held in accordance with the provisions of the Law on Counter-Terrorism of 2005 and subsequent amendments and reasonable grounds held in accordance with the Law against an undetectable weapons, may not breach Article 8 of the Europe- coloured Convention. This interpretation sought from the Court clarifies the boundaries of this Article and enhances the protection resulting therefrom of human rights holders subject to the law itself. However, it is also pertinent that those in power must act reasonably and in proportion".

An individual's right to peaceful assembly, as enshrined in Article 11 of the European Convention on Human Rights, was violated when police disproportionate to the circumstances obstructing a protest in a public square, which had drawn hundreds of participants protesting the government's recent decision on environmental protection legislation, as it restricted the individuals' freedom of expression.

The applicant, a Polish national, sought judicial review of a decision by the Municipal Department of Social Care, Warsaw, through which her parental authority was revoked without any valid grounds. Unsatisfied, she turned to the Second Tier Tribunal of the Court of Appeal of Warsaw, which dismissed her request based on the fact that not enough grounds had been put forward for annulment. A direct appeal to the General Court of Warsaw was also rejected after it concluded there were no grounds to negate the prior decisions. Consequently, the applicant further pursued her claim before the European Court of Human Rights, leading to this Court rendering a ruling in her favor after recognizing violations of Article 6 and 12 of the European Convention on Human Rights.

"The European Court of Human Rights has consistently grappled with the complex intersection of free speech and moral values, specifically in the landmark case of 'Bosnia and Herzegovina v. Bulgaria,' in which the Court found that censorship is only justifiable in exceptional circumstances; however, this raises fundamental questions regarding the balancing of competing rights under Article 10 of the European Convention."
The European Court of Human Rights has consistently upheld the fundamental right to freedom of expression, citing previous judgments in similar matters as a precedent in the current case.

"The European Court of Human Rights has once again dealt a significant blow to the rights of workers across the continent, ruling that the recent policy changes in the sector amount to indirect discrimination, and have rendered redundant hundreds of positions in a major company in Ireland.

"In her decision delivered today, the court of justice reiterated the gravity of ensuring that minority rights are not only guaranteed but also effectively enforced, citing a decade-old case that saw Ukrainian asylum-seekers fail in their bid to have their detention under international law reviewed due to insufficient government evidence. The tribunal's ruling served as a stark reminder that the safeguard of human rights, albeit crucial, does not overshadow the necessity of adherence to established procedures and the respect of fundamental individual freedoms, principles emphasized by the ECHR in numerous landmark rulings over the years."

The European Court of Human Rights has consistently recognized the inherent dignity and equality of all individuals before the law, entitling them to the protection of their fundamental rights and freedoms. In stark contrast to the long-held notion that individual rights must be balanced against the collective interests of a nation, the ECHR stands steadfast in its commitment to upholding the universal applicability of human rights. Over the years, the court has demonstrated an unwavering resolve to hold states accountable for their actions, examining the intricate balance between individual rights and state authority in numerous seminal cases. The court's ruling on the case of McCarthy v. The United Kingdom serves as a paradigmatic instance of this unwavering commitment, affirming that it is the duty of states to provide a safeguarded environment for individuals to live free from unwarranted intrusion into private and family life. The ECHR's commitment to human rights has far-reaching implications for the global community, underscoring the importance of national and international cooperation to ensure a unified and cohesive defense of human dignity. By doing so, the court maintains an inviolable firewall against the erosion of individual liberties, safeguarding the principles on which democratic societies are based.

"The European Court of Human Rights must examine whether the expulsion of a Croatian national from the UK constituted a breach of Article 8 of the European Convention, which protects rights and freedoms based on 'family life,' considering the plaintiff's prior residence in-country and the lack of a 'well-founded reason' for his removal. In previous similar cases, similar reasoning has led to the determination of such allegations that an individual's inherent dignity and privacy must not be disregarded."

In a landmark decision, the ECHR ruled that the blanket ban on headscarves in French schools was a thinly veiled attempt to stifle religious freedom. The court found that the restriction on hijab-clad pupils' educational enrichment to be in breach of Article 2 of the European Convention on Human Rights, which acknowledges the right to non-discrimination and equal treatment.

The European Court of Human Rights has found that the applicant's right to a fair trial had not been respected, as the prosecution had failed to disclose a crucial piece of evidence, despite it being within its possession, thereby allowing the accused to access crucial information to mount an effective defense, and the application was thus successful.

"In the highly publicized case of Bogdanov v. Russia, the European Court of Human Rights issued a landmark ruling, striking down the Ukrainian language law, which restricted non-Russians to use the Russian language in the courts.
The European Court of Human Rights yesterday emphasized the importance of environmental standards in the UK.

In the case of Kudla v. Russia, the European Court of Human Rights found that Russia's expulsion of Russian citizen Andrei Romanov, a former minister, in 2003, in retaliation for his advocacy against the Nagorno-Karabakh conflict, violated Article 6 § 1 of the European Convention on Human Rights. The Court stated that "[i]t is for the domestic government to find the accused liable for the adverse consequences of its conduct."

The Court of Grand Chamber of the European Court of Human Rights, sitting in a session on May 15, 2023, ruled in the case of X v. Republic of Hungary that the Hungarian government's provision of free online education and digital literacy training was in direct violation of Article 3 (Freedom of expression) of the European Convention on Human Rights, citing its obligation to ensure the free flow of information across its borders, while also protecting such access for vulnerable populations through various channels of access.

The Court of Human Rights Commission made a landmark ruling in the Case of Sarkandar H.A. Selimisyan v. Turkey, affirming the prohibition on Turkey's deployment of live ammunition in urban areas and necessically making civilian casualties a violation of Article 2, Section 1 of the European Convention on Human Rights.

The European Court of Human Rights case of Ash v. Turkey highlighted the importance of considering the right to freedom of expression in the context of press freedom. The court in this 1973 case established that an individual's ability to express their thoughts and opinions through the press is crucial to the functioning of a democratic society, provided the expression does not incite violence or violate the rights of others. This fundamental principle has been consistently upheld by the ECHR in subsequent cases, emphasizing the need for a balance between protecting individual freedoms and the greater good of society as a whole.

"The ECHR, an independent judicial body of the Council of Europe, has issued a significant ruling yesterday in a landmark case regarding the right to equality before the law and non-discrimination, reaffirming its commitment to upholding the fundamental principles of human rights as enshrined in the European Convention on Human Rights, whilst also emphasizing the need for greater vigilance in preventing discriminatory practices and ensuring equal treatment for all persons within member states."
During a landmark case that would later become a landmark judicial decision, the European Court of Human Rights (ECHR) addressed the complex issue of freedom of expression in the digital age.

The European Court of Human Rights has ruled that the British government must provide adequate assistance to individuals who, while serving their prison sentences, are also being subjected to torture in other countries, a step which the applicant had been seeking for the past thirteen years. The majority of the court agreed that the harm suffered by the applicant also met the definition of inhuman and degrading treatment because the applicant endured an extended period of inhuman and degrading treatment when the prison authorities deprived the applicant from consulting his lawyer, although the court ruled that the applicant was adequately rehabilitated and his risk of re-entry of criminal, violent behaviour was low.

"The European Court's landmark decision in the case of X v. Y established a crucial precedent, affirming the right to privacy as enshrined in Article 8 of the European Convention on Human Rights, and illuminating the delicate balance between individual freedoms and state surveillance practices."
The European Court of Human Rights, having heard the case of Ms. Kostadinova v. Bulgaria (app. No. 36833/07), delivered its judgment on 24 July 2008.

In a landmark ruling, the ECHR has struck down a provision in the UK's asylum laws, deeming it incompatible with European Convention rights. The court found that the current process of processing asylum claims can lead to detention of claimants for extended periods, resulting in arbitrary treatment.

10.0: The applicant, while en route to visit her sick child via bus 101, was refused entry to the city of Strasbourg because the bus company, despite her request for accommodations at the time of booking, had not booked a wheelchair-accessible vehicle, citing transport schedules had actually concluded. However, in view of her permanent disability and the difficulties in commuting without this, she sought compensation under the contract.

"The European Court of Human Rights has acknowledged the right to freedom of expression is not absolute, and the restriction of this right is justified when it is necessary in a democratic society for the protection of other fundamental freedoms, as seen in the landmark case of Handyside v. United Kingdom (1979)."

An individual's right to effective remand into pre-trial scrutiny and protection against abuse of power was vindicated in Yudin v. Russia, where the European Court of Human Rights found the government had breached article 6 § 1 of the Convention by failing to afford bail upon prompt arrest, given mitigating personal and material conditions.

"The European Court of Human Rights has consistently upheld the fundamental right to fair trial guarantees, affirming that the principle of objectivity in the assessment of guilt lies within the remit of judicial competence - the Court ruled in a landmark finding that an open-ended presumption of guilt solely based on circumstantial evidence disregarded due process rights."

"The European Court of Human Rights, in a landmark decision, ruled in favor of the individual who claimed that the state's failure to provide adequate linguistic support in prison had rendered the right to effective communication under Article 6 of the European Convention fundamentally incompatible with their human rights. However, the court's reasoning highlighted the need for member states to balance the duty to ensure language accessibility with the significant resources required to establish specialized interpreters and linguists necessary to accommodate linguistic diversity, a conundrum that has plagued the institution for years."

"In recent years, the European Court of Human Rights has seen an uptick in applications from Ukrainian nationals alleging human rights violations in Poland, often stemming from perceived discriminatory treatment and restricted freedom of assembly during public protests. These cases frequently revolve around Article 11, which protects freedom of assembly, suggesting a growing tension between governmental restrictions and the fundamental right to peaceful protest. A notable case, 'The Scottish Busworkers' Union,' involved Scottish and Norwegian national activists seeking acknowledgment of their inherent right to peaceful assembly under Article 11, amidst heightened scrutiny from the court's European Community law interpretations relating to trade union regulations."

"In relation to the grounds of violating Article 2 of the Convention, the Court underscored that the measures taken to locate and convict Marian K., despite numerous leads and significant discoveries, could not satisfy the required standard of diligence to initiate criminal proceedings within a reasonable and prescribed time."

The ECHR has consistently demonstrated its commitment to upholding the principles enshrined in the European Convention on Human Rights, as enshrined in Article 5 (Right to liberty and security) and Article 6 (Right to a fair trial), which have shaped the course of numerous landmark cases, including N.J. v. the United Kingdom and Mathieu-Marty v. France, in which the court has scrutinized the detention and treatment of vulnerable individuals. However, the Court has also showcased its capacity to balance these rights with the need for law enforcement and judicial authority, exemplified by the case of Geduldig v. the United Kingdom, which involved the authority of the court to require state evidence.

The European Court of Human Rights has consistently ruled that freedom of expression also encompasses the right to mock public figures. In a recent decision, the court upheld the conviction of a Turkish dissident who had insulted the Turkish President.

"The European Court of Human Rights recently ruled in a landmark decision, striking down an EU directive that critics had argued unfairly targeted non-EU migrants. The court's president acknowledged that the clause allowed for arbitrary detention of transnational fugitives but emphasized that the stated criteria were intended to safeguard national security interests."

"In yesterday's ruling, the presiding court found that the respondent state's failure to provide adequate language access services to plaintiff A had violated the European Convention on Human Rights, emphasizing the importance of ensuring that individuals with limited English proficiency can access justice without prejudice or disadvantage."

The Grand Chamber of the European Court of Human Rights found, in the case of Dedeoglu v. Turkey, that Article 10 of the European Convention on Human Rights is applicable to acts which stigmatize or incite hatred against a group recognized by law or by European Community law, as well as against a national minority which, as a consequence, remains at the margin of the public life of that country.

The notion of public interest in the editorial content of a newspaper is an aspect of freedom of expression protected under Article 10 of the European Convention on Human Rights, which obliges member states to strike a balance between the need for the restriction of that freedom for the prevention of disorder or incitement of hatred, and the need to safeguard the rights and freedoms set forth in Article 10 including, inter alia, freedom of expression, on the other hand.

"The Court, pursuant to Article 52 of its Rules of Procedure, finds that Ireland has failed to comply with the European Convention on Human Rights, marking a significant deviation from established jurisprudence."
The European Court's decision on the matter of an individual's right to equal pay despite belonging to rival nationalities raises concerns about discrimination in the workplace.

In its decision in Bosphorus Helsinki Service Ltd. v. Russia, the ECHR confirmed the significance of online freedom of expression in article 10 of the European Convention on Human Rights, stating that the blanket prohibition of YouTube under the Russian 'Fake News' act would infringe article 10 and breach the principles of pluralism and transparency, highlighting the need for governments to adopt more nuanced and proportionate approaches to manage online disinformation.

"The European Court of Human Rights has once again weighed in on the contentious issue of free speech versus national security, ruling unanimously in Case 1543/11 that the imposition of blanket bans on criticism of certain public officials violates an individual's right to freedom of expression under Article 10 of the European Convention. The court found that such blanket bans, while well-intentioned, amount to an 'obviously and manifest' restriction of the expression of opinions, and that less intrusive means must be employed to achieve the desired policy objectives, lest the fundamental right be deemed incompatible with the Constitution.

The applicant, a Polish national, brought an application alleging unlawful detention in Italy, in which he claimed that his detention was merely incidental to lawful purposes and did not sufficiently adhere to the principles established in _Baraganin v. Russia_ (_2018) 62 EHRR 55. Meanwhile, the applicant had submitted a grievance that the police were slow to respond to his complaint, with a delay of several weeks between his reporting the incident and the recording of a police report; this delay, the applicant asserted, tainted the reliability of the subsequent findings. The Court observed that it remained to be seen whether the applicant had fulfilled the formal requirements under Article 35 of the ECHR.

In the esteemed annals of international human rights jurisprudence, the European Court of Human Rights (ECHR) has consistently demonstrated its unwavering commitment to upholding the fundamental principles enshrined in the European Convention on Human Rights.

"The applicant, a 35-year-old Romanian woman, brought a complaint to the Court alleging that the Polish authorities had denied her the right to freedom of expression when they confiscated her homemade signs and proceeded with the demolition of the property where she displayed them, with the arguments being that they were deemed to be in breach of the public order."

There was a marked disparity between the defendant's conduct and the requisite standard of reasonableness, it having been established that the law enforcement officials involved in the detention had failed to provide its particulars and its necessity. The alleged preventive measure did not strike a fair balance between the competing interests of security and liberty and had potentially compromised the rights of the applicant and others, such as the right to freedom of movement.

The determination ofwhether or not there is some state interference which falls within the 'case law of the Cortina type' appears complex in this matter, but if the Court is to find another ground upon which to decide the case, it must consider the right of the claimants, having lodged an application in respect of which a decision awaits in Art. 15 of the Convention, to have their complaints examined by a independent and impartial tribunal established by law. The claimants further contend that the domestic authorities failed to ensure the proper examination of their application and/or refused them reasonable access to an effective remedy, thereby failing to secure them an effective right of access to a tribunal, the consideration of the ECHR being by the Court itself and the State on its sole responsibility, in conformity with Article 13 to the Convention.
The ECHR has increasingly scrutinized the extent to which the rights guaranteed by the European Convention on Human Rights are being breached in the Member States.

The European Court of Human Rights has received an urgent appeal from a young Scottish citizen, Ms. Emily Wilson, who claims that her detention by British security forces in 2023 for solely circulating on social media critical content of the UK's COVID-19 policies breached her right to freedom of association as protected under Article 11 of the ECHR.

In the matter of A and B v. United Kingdom, the European Court of Human Rights ruled that the failure to provide adequate support and assistance to Lone Mothers who were denied housing by public landlords was a breach of Article 3, Section 3 of the European Convention on Human Rights. Despite their best efforts, the parties involved did not agree on an acceptable compromise, and the application was dismissed. Notwithstanding the judges' conclusion, the applicants' concerns continue to resonate, highlighting deeper issues surrounding the treatment of lone mothers in European society.

European Court of Human Rights, Strasbourg, 9 December 2019: The European Court of Human Rights today ruled that by installing a smart door lock, Mr. G, the landlord had failed to accommodate Mr. G’s disability, in violation of Article 9 of the European Convention on Human Rights.

"Impression that there has been a breach of Article 3 through the treatment of Ms. J [case name], where the respondent state detained her for 42 days without a reasonable grounds on suspicion, merely on the basis of certain words used on social media. Article 3 specifically prohibits inhuman and degrading treatment or punishment, warranting utmost scrutiny; given the absence of any tangible evidence linking the detained individual to the incident, the detention bore severe implications on her physical and mental well-being. This raises essential questions surrounding the exercise of detention powers in accordance with Article 8, safeguarding the proportionality and necessary grounds for every arrest, detention, or questioning; ultimately, addressing concerns central to ensuring the state upholds the fundamental freedoms outlined in the Convention."

In light of the landmark case of Mathieu-Maggi v. France (2002), the European Court of Human Rights reaffirmed the importance of private life in Article 8 of the European Convention, stating that the threshold of a 'genuine and sincere interest' is subjective and can vary from one individual to another.

The European Court of Human Rights has consistently demonstrated its commitment to the principles of human rights and the rule of law, as seen in the ruling of Kruz et al. in the Application No. 10871/09, a case that shed light on the importance of ensuring access to effective rehabilitation programs for individuals convicted of crimes against humanity.

The European Court of Human Rights has yet again been compelled to address a landmark case that has significant implications for the rights of marginalized communities. The dispute, which revolves around a long-standing issue in the town of Ravenswood, concerns the expansion of local businesses onto land previously owned by a family of indigenous descent. The family, in opposition to these developments, claimed that the construction would encroach upon their ancestral heritage, displacing them from their ancestral grounds and threatening their very identity.

The Committee adopted a finding earlier today that by failing to provide adequate notice of the planned demolition work, the Hungarian authorities breached article 6 paragraph one of the Convention. However, President of the European Court of Human Rights, Kunneke reckons Hungary could potentially win, whilst stating that the council must carry out necessary investigations.

In a landmark case that has garnered widespread attention for its bold statement on human rights, the European Court of Human Rights weighed in on the matter, citing precedence set by previous decisions while adhering to the core principles of human dignity and legal precedent.
The European Court of Human Rights has refused a complaint from a Polish man whose 12-year-old son was arrested after he allegedly vandalized school property using a correction fluid.

"The ECHR has consistently reiterated that Article 3 of the European Convention on Human Rights must be strictly interpreted in a manner that prioritizes the prevention of torture, bearing in mind the inherent dignity of all individuals and the imperative to protect the most vulnerable members of our society, in this case, the applicants who in the darkest corners of the continent risk being silenced, and yet their stories will be heard."

The European court of human rights heard a landmark case yesterday, in which a former employee of the UK's Metropolitan Police Service lodged a formal complaint against the government, alleging systematic discrimination and unlawful procedures in their treatment of whistleblowers.

"The long-standing case of Z. vs. the United Kingdom, which centered on the issue of the right to a fair hearing by an independent and impartial tribunal established by law, has far-reaching implications for individuals who claim their liberty may be breached under the European Convention on Human Rights. The court acknowledged that the State's actions were motivated by a desire to avert chaos through a peaceful means, but found that the Polish authorities had still failed to strike a fair balance between the two competing rights.

The European Court of Human Rights has affirmed the judgment of the French court that a Muslim woman, denied access to an abortion mid-trimester due to the 2010 constitutional provision banning the procedure before the 12th week of pregnancy, was not afforded a justifiable discrimination under Article 10 of the Convention, but Article 6 had not been breached as the French system offered alternatives.

In a decision delivered on 7 June 2019 in the case of Petrovic v. Croatia, the European Court of Human Rights held that the legislation at issue infringed the applicant's right to freedom of expression under Article 10 of the European Convention on Human Rights.

The European Court of Humane Sentences recently issued a landmark ruling in the case of Smirnov v. Russia, finding that the rejection of a gay couple's application for a marriage license was discriminatory and incompatible with the European Convention on Human Rights.

"The applicants commenced proceedings against the Republic of Bulgaria alleging violations of article 8 in conjunction with article 10 of the European Convention, stemming from the denial of access to information regarding the status of persons requesting asylum from Ukraine following the Ukrainian-Russian conflict, resulting in self-imposed detention until their applications were processed."

According to the European Court of Human Rights, the government's failure to provide adequate medical treatment for Ms. K. in emergency circumstances constitutes a violation of her right to life under Article 2 of the European Convention on Human Rights, as recognized by Judgement No. 67892/20, dated March 11, 2021.

"In the seminal case of Mclauchlan v. United Kingdom (2001), the European Court of Human Rights (ECHR) extensively examined the European Convention on Human Rights' Article 6(3)(e), specifically addressing the issue of anonymous sources in inquiries. The case took place in a jurisdiction where an anonymous source, known only as 'M', had disclosed factual information regarding a state agent's involvement in the disappearance of an individual. The Court ultimately ruled that such anonymous sources, under the provisions of Article 6(3)(e), could be admissible in the investigation process, permitting the inquiry to be conducted despite the lack of direct evidence. This landmark decision highlighted the intricate balance between the need for a thorough investigation and the rights of individuals to remain anonymous."

The European Court of Human Rights, in its landmark decision in the case of X v. The United Kingdom, clarified the notion of a 'reasonable request' under Article 2 of the European Convention on Human Rights. The applicant, an 8-year-old girl, had been left at a Bulgarian border crossing when her parents were detained for questioning by Romanian authorities.

"The European Court of Human Rights has consistently upheld the right to property under Article 1 of the European Convention, as codified in the ECHR, in a string of landmark decisions, most notably in the landmark case of Stepramsky v. Russia, where the court held that the seizure of a former Soviet dissident's property without fair compensation constituted a violation of the right to property, yet the court also consistently acknowledged the need to balance this right with the need to prevent the misuse of property in the interests of national security, evident in the subsequent adjudication of Slants SpA v. Moldova, where the ECHR upheld the seizure of a frozen Estonian bank account in response to the frozen assets of an individual accused of espionage and embezzlement for the benefit of an unauthorized foreign entity, rendering Moldova's attempts to seize the funds unlawful without due process and compelling evidence."

The European Court of Human Rights has repeatedly emphasized the importance of balancing individual rights with the need for legitimate government medidas in maintaining law and order, as in the case of _T.G. v. the United Kingdom._ In its judgment, the court highlighted the complexity of striking such a balance, often highlighting the imperfections of the initial UK assessment in certain circumstances.

"The European Court of Human Rights, like the Supreme Court of the United States, serves as the highest authority in the judicial systems of many European countries, offering remedies to individuals whose human rights have been violated by the actions of their governments or public authorities. Germany case where a Hungarian national's imprisonment was deemed unlawful detention."

Russia, dated 31 July 2014, is a landmark ruling on the application of Article 39 of the European Convention on Human Rights, which ensures the right to free and fair election processes. The applicant, Mr. Kasimov, lodged a complaint with the European Court of Human Rights, alleging that in the Russian State Duma elections held in 2011, his vote was not counted in the tally due to discrepancies in the ballot papers his family had been caring for. The Court found that the Russian authorities had breached the right to free and fair elections in ensuring the veracity of the vote count procedure. The judgment established that the election irregularities gave rise to a perceived lack of electoral integrity and undermined the legitimacy of the stated outcome.
